responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a structural digital-sovereignty risk: MitID and broader citizen access to critical public services depend on Apple and Google's proprietary platform controls, trust signals, and distribution infrastructure. The project's core tension is between institutional inertia (existing operators resist platform-neutral alternatives citing integration cost and convenience) and the urgency of establishing fallback mechanisms before the eIDAS2 wallet (AltID) hardens the same dependencies further. Success requires three linked interventions: (1) reframing the problem from ideology to resilience and procurement risk, (2) producing credible technical evidence that standards-based fallback models are feasible, and (3) using coalition leverage and EU-level pressure to embed platform-neutrality language into procurement and standards before institutional lock-in becomes irreversible. The selected levers navigate the trade-off between radical platform replacement (unrealistic) and incremental tinkering (insufficient), focusing instead on procurement intervention, coalition-building momentum, and demonstrator credibility as drivers of systemic change.

levers.name: Reframing vector: resilience and contingency versus ideology
levers.consequences: By leading with supplier-concentration risk, continuity-of-service exposure, and procurementauthority interests rather than privacy or geopolitical narratives, the project gains traction with civil-protection, treasury, and digital-governance stakeholders who control infrastructure decisions. This reframe also deflects incumbent resistance framed as "ideological" and anchors the case in measurable institutional risk. The trade-off is that resilience framing may dilute the privacy and democratic-accountability case, potentially leaving MitID's structural surveillance characteristics unaddressed in final procurement requirements.
options: Lead all phase-one messaging with quantified supplier-concentration risk (e.g., 'two vendors control authentication for 98% of citizen-critical services'), targeting treasury, contingency planning, and digital-governance officials before engaging civil-society advocates.

Develop dual messaging: one for institutional and procurement audiences emphasizing resilience and fallback requirements, and a separate narrative for civil society and media emphasizing privacy, democratic accountability, and reduced lock-in, coordinated through the coalition.

Position the issue primarily through the lens of eIDAS2 compliance and EU-mandated interoperability, framing platform-neutrality as a technical requirement derived from Brussels rather than a Danish political demand.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is whether reframing toward resilience weakens the privacy and sovereignty argument by making it seem merely technical rather than politically significant. The options all assume that institutional and civil-society audiences can be reached simultaneously without contradiction; in practice, emphasizing contingency planning and supplier risk to treasury officials may inadvertently signal to incumbent operators that platform-neutral fallbacks are merely insurance—nice-to-have rather than mandatory—which undercuts procurement pressure.
levers.lever_classification: governance — which narrative framing drives political and institutional buy-in

levers.name: Coalition composition and advocacy sequencing
levers.consequences: The project's ability to embed platform-neutrality language into procurement and standards depends entirely on demonstrating that multiple constituencies (civil society, academic security experts, contingency planners, sympathetic legislators, and EU-aligned policy makers) view the issue as serious. Early coalition building (phase one) establishes credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operators before phase three procurement intervention. The trade-off is that broader coalitions are slower to mobilize and may dilute the project's technical and policy message to accommodate diverse stakeholder interests.
options: Build a narrow coalition of elite security researchers, privacy advocates, and one or two forward-thinking Folketinget members during phase one, then rapidly expand public visibility in phase two using demonstrators and media to create political pressure before phase three procurement engagement.

Establish a formal, multi-stakeholder governance board (including civil-society organizations, academic institutions, industry associations, and government observers) from month one, prioritizing consensus-building over rapid action to signal legitimacy and institutional endorsement to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

Sequence advocacy through existing Danish digital-governance committees and EU-level working groups first, allowing institutional channels to build momentum before civil-society mobilization, thereby reducing perceived radicalism and increasing adoption likelihood in procurement.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between insider legitimacy (formal boards, institutional channels) and disruptive visibility (media, public pressure). None of the options adequately address the risk that a slow, consensus-oriented coalition will find MitID operators and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen unmotivated to change before AltID architecture is finalized; conversely, a fast external campaign may be dismissed as activism rather than credible technical input.
levers.lever_classification: governance — which stakeholder groups are mobilized and in what sequence

levers.name: Demonstrator scope and proof-of-concept boundaries
levers.consequences: Non-production demonstrators (phase two) serve as credible evidence that standards-based, platform-neutral authentication is technically feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google. Demonstrators against simulated environments, using open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect, GrapheneOS), provide the technical ammunition for phase three procurement language. The trade-off is that demonstrators remain deliberately non-production; if institutions perceive them as incomplete prototypes rather than feasibility evidence, they may be dismissed as insufficient to guide real procurement decisions.
options: Develop a single, comprehensive mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS that closely mirrors real-world MitID interaction patterns, including encryption, multi-factor support, and mock integration with simulated banking and tax-filing portals, accepting increased scope and timeline risk to maximize credibility.

Produce two lean, modular demonstrators—one mobile-based FIDO2/OpenID stack on GrapheneOS and one browser-based WebAuthn fallback—each deliberately kept minimal to confirm feasibility and reduce complexity, emphasizing generalizability over feature completeness.

Commission an external, independent security audit of the demonstrator architecture and source code to certify that standards-based approaches meet Substantial-level eIDAS requirements, prioritizing third-party validation over in-house implementation.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between demonstrator completeness (which increases institutional credibility but risks scope creep and delays) and demonstrator minimalism (which speeds delivery but may be perceived as toys or proofs-of-concept rather than evidence for procurement decisions). None of the options squarely address whether demonstrators should attempt certification-level assurance or remain deliberately pre-certification, which affects how seriously institutions take the findings.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how demonstrator scope and validation rigor are balanced

levers.name: AltID engagement strategy and timing of procurement intervention
levers.consequences: Phase three success hinges on embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into AltID procurement, standards, or design documents. Timing is critical: AltID architecture is likely hardening in 2027–2028, creating a narrow window for influence. Early engagement (phase two) establishes relationships and surfaces obstacles; late engagement (phase three) allows demonstrators to inform procurement but risks missing architecture-lock-in. The trade-off is between establishing early trust with AltID stakeholders (which may constrain the project's ability to be critical) and waiting for demonstrator credibility (which may arrive too late to influence design).
options: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership as formal project partners from month six onward, involving them in demonstrator design and phase-one findings, positioning the project as supportive infrastructure rather than external pressure.

Maintain deliberate distance during phases one and two, building independent credibility through publications and coalition, then enter AltID procurement discussions in month 18 with demonstrators, feasibility reports, and EU-aligned standards language as external leverage rather than insider collaboration.

Parallel-path engagement: establish informal working relationships with AltID technical teams and EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups during phase two, sharing findings in non-binding forums while preserving the formal independence and public coalition for phase three pressure.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is whether early partnership risks co-optation (AltID leadership absorbs recommendations without binding commitment) or early distance risks irrelevance (decisions are already made by the time external pressure arrives). None of the options adequately account for the likelihood that AltID procurement cycles are already determined and that formal change requests may face automatic rejection unless political pressure from multiple directions (EU, civil society, treasury) converges simultaneously.
levers.lever_classification: governance — timing and leverage model for procurement influence

levers.name: EU-level standards alignment and European Digital Identity Wallet positioning
levers.consequences: Aligning the project's platform-neutrality advocacy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards and eIDAS2 interoperability requirements amplifies Danish influence by embedding the case in supranational regulation rather than relying on Danish institutional change alone. EU-level engagement also creates precedent for other member states and reduces the perception that platform-neutral fallbacks are idiosyncratic Danish demands. The trade-off is that EU-level standards work moves slowly (24–36 month cycles for formal amendments) and may be co-opted by incremental language that does not mandate fallback mechanisms.
options: Secure direct engagement with the European Commission's digital identity working groups and the EIDAS2 standards revision process by month twelve, submitting technical input on platform-neutral access and fallback authentication, treating the EU as the primary intervention point and positioning Denmark as implementing supranational requirements.

Develop a joint position paper with other Nordic or EU member states expressing shared concerns about platform lock-in in digital identity, building multilateral coalition pressure before approaching Brussels, thereby increasing influence on EU standards development.

Produce a standalone technical report on platform-neutral fallback authentication aligned to eIDAS Substantial-level requirements, publish it through academic and EU policy channels, and allow it to circulate as independent evidence without formal project branding, permitting broader adoption and reducing institutional resistance.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between direct supranational influence (which requires sustained EU-level presence and relationships) and ambient diffusion of technical evidence (which spreads ideas but lacks coordination). None of the options adequately address whether EU-level engagement distracts from Danish institutional change or strengthens it; there is also a risk that EU standards language remains purely aspirational and does not translate into binding procurement requirements at member-state level.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how European standards and supranational regulatory frameworks are leveraged

levers.name: Financing contingency and phase-gating discipline
levers.consequences: The project budget (10.5 million DKK across three phases) is front-loaded risk: phase one funding is unconditional, but phases two and three are conditional on documented progress and institutional engagement. This creates forced decision points and prevents funding drawdown on weak results. Stringent gating discipline prevents scope creep and money burning on low-probability outcomes. The trade-off is that conditional funding may starve phase two and three if phase one produces incomplete coalition buy-in or if AltID engagement remains informal, leaving demonstrators unfinished and standards language unachieved.
options: Establish a formal, externally chaired project review board at month eight (end of phase one) that makes a binding decision whether phases two and three are funded based on documented feasibility evidence, institutional engagement, and coalition strength; if conditions are not met, wind down the project and publish findings.

Secure the full 10.5 million DKK in phase one as a single conditional budget contingent on phase one deliverables, allowing internal project management to reallocate funds across phases two and three based on institutional momentum, reducing external gate reviews but increasing execution risk.

Structure funding as three tranches released against specific phase-exit criteria (phase one: published feasibility report and two formal institutional engagement letters; phase two: two working demonstrators and documented AltID/EU engagement; phase three: procurement or standards language inclusion), with a clear wind-down protocol if gates are not met.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between adaptive funding flexibility (which allows pivoting toward unexpected opportunities) and disciplined contingency planning (which prevents resource waste on dead-end paths). None of the options directly address what happens if institutional inertia defeats the project's goals by month 20—whether the project pivots toward publication and EU-level influence or admits failure and closes. The current framing assumes conditional gates will transparently reveal dead ends; in practice, institutional stakeholders may provide ambiguous signals that allow the project to continue despite weak progress.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how funding releases and phase-gating discipline constrain execution

levers.name: Risk escalation and incumbent operator response strategy
levers.consequences: MitID operators and platform vendors (Apple, Google) have strong incentives to resist platform-neutral alternatives: platform-neutral fallbacks imply reduced exclusive dependence on their distribution, trust signals, and telemetry. By month ten or twelve, operators may become actively obstructive—withholding interoperability information, lobbying against procurement changes, or arguing that fallback authentication introduces compliance risk or user-experience degradation. The project must anticipate and prepare for escalation, including media campaigns, technical counter-arguments, and regulatory lobbying from incumbents. Failure to prepare a public-response and escalation strategy leaves the project vulnerable to being out-maneuvered during phase three.
options: Prepare a detailed 'incumbent response playbook' during phase two that documents likely operator objections (cost, compatibility, security assurance), pre-authors rebuttals, and briefs coalition members on counter-messaging, ensuring rapid, coordinated public response if operators launch campaigns against platform-neutrality procurement language.

Engage MitID operators as early as phase one through formal consultation channels, inviting their input on demonstrator design and fallback-access requirements, positioning platform-neutral fallbacks as supplementary rather than threatening, and reducing likelihood of active escalation through perceived co-optation of their concerns.

Assume active incumbent resistance and design phase three engagement to bypass MitID operators entirely, focusing procurement pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership through political and EU channels rather than attempting consensus with existing platform-dependent vendors.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between consensus-building with incumbents (which reduces overt resistance but may be perceived as capitulation) and public escalation (which mobilizes coalition and media support but risks unified operator pushback that derails procurement influence). None of the options adequately account for the possibility that incumbents will pre-empt the project by accelerating AltID integration with their own platforms, making fallback architectures moot by the time phase three procurement pressure begins.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how the project anticipates and responds to incumbent operator resistance

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a structural supplier-concentration risk: MitID's trust model depends on Apple and Google's platform integrity signals, creating both sovereignty and continuity exposure. The project's core tension is that retrofitting MitID is impractical, but influencing AltID and EU standards before the identity stack ossifies is feasible and high-leverage. The selected levers address this by targeting three interdependent decision points: (1) how to reframe the issue from ideology to risk management and procurement neutrality; (2) which technical demonstrators will convince skeptics without overreach; (3) how to coordinate timing between Danish procurement intervention and EU standards alignment. Success requires balancing credibility (rigorous technical work, institutional engagement) against political momentum (coalition strength, standards pressure) while managing the incumbent's predictable resistance and the temptation to expand scope beyond what phase budgets can sustain.

levers.name: Institutional entry vector and stakeholder sequencing
levers.consequences: Deciding whether to approach Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, AltID governance, or EU bodies first shapes both the speed of formal engagement and the likelihood of blocking from incumbents. Early contact with MitID operators may expose the project's intent prematurely and trigger defensive positioning; early EU engagement offers diplomatic cover but delays Danish procurement leverage. This lever determines whether the project operates as a peer-to-peer researcher engagement, a formal proposal through official channels, or a standards advocacy campaign from the outside in.
options: Initiate direct conversation with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's digital-identity working group early in phase one, framing the engagement as resilience planning and continuity risk assessment rather than MitID criticism, with the goal of securing an explicit request for the feasibility report.

Approach AltID governance and procurement teams in parallel with phase-two demonstrator work, positioning the fallback authentication concept as a risk-mitigation requirement for AltID's design rather than a separate competing pathway.

Prioritize EU-level engagement through the European Digital Identity Wallet working group and relevant standards bodies (ETSI, CEN) during phase two and three, allowing Danish framing to feed into broader European interoperability and resilience language before Danish procurement finalizes.
levers.review_lever: This lever assumes that institutional receptivity is stable across the 24-month window, but digital-policy cycles are compressed and government priorities shift with electoral cycles. None of the options account for a sudden political reshuffle that could either accelerate or freeze the entire Danish digital-identity agenda, nor do they address the risk that early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen could trigger an 'internal review' that delays any formal response for 12+ months.
levers.lever_classification: governance — which institutional door to knock on first and in what sequence

levers.name: Platform-neutrality requirement scope and procurement language
levers.consequences: Deciding whether to push for 'no exclusive dependency on Apple or Google,' 'mandatory platform-agnostic fallback paths,' or a broader 'supplier diversity and continuity' mandate determines how aggressively the project constrains future identity-stack design. Narrow wording may be easier to negotiate but does not prevent AltID from choosing a single certified platform; broad wording generates stronger resistance from incumbents and may fail at procurement gate. This lever directly shapes what 'success' looks like in phase three and whether the project claims a real policy win or a symbolic endorsement.
options: Draft language requiring that Danish digital identity systems must not depend exclusively on any single commercial platform provider or app-store ecosystem for distribution, integrity attestation, or push-notification delivery, with a mandatory platform-neutral fallback for critical public-sector authentication.

Propose a weaker formulation focusing on 'supplier resilience' and 'continuity planning' that does not explicitly name Apple or Google but creates procurement incentives to evaluate alternative distribution and trust models without mandating exclusion.

Advocate for an EU-wide interoperability and portability standard (aligned with European Digital Identity Wallet requirements) that requires all member-state identity wallets to support migration between platforms and certified fallback mechanisms, making Danish requirements part of a collective European standard rather than a unilateral Danish constraint.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between procurement enforceability and political feasibility. Narrow language is easier to pass but creates loopholes (e.g., 'fallback' can be lip-service, or AltID can comply by adding a weak browser option). Broad language is harder to negotiate and may face legal challenge from affected platforms. None of the options address the post-approval implementation gap: even if language is included in procurement, the operator may interpret 'platform-neutral' minimally or design a fallback so cumbersome that take-up is negligible.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how stringently to constrain digital identity procurement to platform-neutral and fallback-capable design

levers.name: Demonstrator credibility and certification scope
levers.consequences: Choosing whether demonstrators use production-grade cryptographic libraries and certified components, prototype-grade open-source implementations, or hybrid approaches affects both the technical credibility with skeptics and the implementation timeline. Full certification is impractical within budget and timeline, but too-academic demonstrators will be dismissed as disconnected from operational reality. This lever shapes whether phase-two work produces compelling evidence that platform-neutral design is immediately deployable or merely 'theoretically feasible someday.'
options: Build demonstrators using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on production-validated cryptographic libraries and hardware security modules (HSM), with external security review by an accredited firm, stopping short of formal certification but generating independent audit reports that skeptics cannot dismiss as incomplete.

Develop a lean prototype on GrapheneOS and open-source authentication components, explicitly labeling it as a proof-of-concept and leveraging academic partnerships for peer review rather than commercial certification, emphasizing feasibility and design transparency over production-readiness.

Combine a mobile-oriented demonstrator using commercial-grade FIDO2 stacks with a browser-based fallback using a hardware security token (e.g., FIDO2 USB key) managed by an established digital-identity provider, demonstrating that fallback works with existing procurement-approved suppliers and requires no new certification infrastructure.
levers.review_lever: All three options assume that external review (academic, commercial, or token-provider-backed) will convince procurement officers, but procurement decisions are often driven by political comfort and path-dependency, not technical credibility alone. A well-audited demonstrator may still lose to 'incumbent supplier already certified and operationally supported.' The options also do not address the risk that showcasing a working fallback will shift incumbent pressure from 'fallback is impossible' to 'fallback is a security liability and must not be enabled in production.'
levers.lever_classification: execution — how robust and independently validated to make the non-production demonstrators

levers.name: Coalition visibility and advocacy messaging
levers.consequences: Deciding whether the coalition operates openly and loudly, coordinates through back-channel institutional relationships, or alternates visibility based on phase will affect both media attention and incumbent defensiveness. High visibility early pressures procurement decision-makers but also triggers industry counter-lobbying and may be perceived as anti-business; low visibility preserves relationship capital but risks being out-maneuvered by incumbent lobby efforts during phase three. This lever determines whether the project frames platform-neutrality as a civil-rights and sovereignty issue, a technical resilience question, or a procurement risk management problem.
options: Maintain a transparent, publicly-visible coalition with media engagement, academic publications, and civil-society visibility throughout, positioning supplier concentration as a digital-sovereignty and democratic-accountability issue, accepting that incumbents will counter-lobby aggressively but that public pressure will constrain their ability to dismiss the project as fringe.

Operate the coalition in stealth mode during phases one and two, building institutional credibility and demonstrators without public announcement, then escalate visibility and media engagement sharply in phase three when procurement decisions are being finalized, using the accumulation of evidence and institutional endorsements to create a harder-to-ignore late-stage campaign.

Coordinate coalition messaging through sympathetic members of Folketinget and related parliamentary committees, framing the issue as resilience and public-sector strategic autonomy rather than anti-corporate sentiment, using parliamentary channels and consultations as the primary advocacy mechanism while keeping external coalition activity low-key.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between agenda-setting power and incumbent retaliation. High visibility creates political cover but also gives opponents time to prepare counterarguments and lobby. Back-channel and parliamentary strategies risk being outmaneuvered if incumbents have stronger political access. None of the options adequately address the risk that a late-stage media escalation (option two) could be characterized as ambush lobbying by opponents, damaging the project's credibility.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — whether the coalition operates visibly or through institutional channels, and how to frame the advocacy message

levers.name: Fallback authentication pathway architecture and complexity
levers.consequences: Choosing between a mobile-OS-independent browser-based authentication flow, a dedicated hardware token approach (FIDO2 USB), or a hybrid that requires users to have multiple authentication options determines both the operational usability of any fallback and the likelihood that end-users will actually use it under stress. A browser-based fallback is user-friendly but still depends on browser vendors and OS-level trust anchors; a hardware token is vendor-agnostic but requires physical distribution and user habit-change. This lever shapes the technical defensibility of the fallback concept and its practical viability if a platform provider degrades service.
options: Design the fallback around a browser-based WebAuthn flow using a server-controlled trust model (public-key pinning and certificate validation independent of OS) that works across any modern browser and device, accepting that browsers still depend on operating-system crypto APIs but reducing exclusive reliance on Apple or Google app-store integrity signals.

Develop a hardware-token-based FIDO2 fallback (USB security key, smart card, or equivalent) that users carry separately and use with any device that has a USB port or NFC reader, eliminating OS-level trust dependencies entirely but requiring out-of-band physical distribution and a user-behavior shift during authentication emergencies.

Build a hybrid approach where critical public-sector authentication defaults to mobile app when available, falls back to browser-based WebAuthn, and falls back further to hardware token, allowing users to authenticate across a graduated spectrum of fallback options while maintaining a single identity certificate across all pathways.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between architectural simplicity and real-world resilience. Browser-based fallback is easier to explain but provides limited sovereignty if browsers themselves become pressure points for geopolitical interference. Hardware tokens eliminate OS dependency but face adoption and operational-complexity barriers in a crisis (how many citizens carry security keys?). The hybrid approach seems comprehensive but introduces orchestration complexity: if the primary app fails, does the user know to try browser, and does the public sector support three authentication pathways in production?
levers.lever_classification: product — which fallback authentication mechanism to prioritize and how to balance usability against vendor independence

levers.name: EU standards alignment timing and negotiation priority
levers.consequences: Deciding whether to frame EU engagement as a lever to influence Danish procurement, a parallel agenda that will inform Danish decisions by osmosis, or a primary goal independent of Danish outcomes determines how much political capital to invest in European working groups versus domestic Danish channels. Early EU framing can create diplomatic cover and broader legitimacy, but EU standards processes are slow and Denmark may move faster; late EU engagement means missing the window for influencing European Digital Identity Wallet architecture. This lever shapes whether the project's success is measured partly by EU-level outcomes or entirely by Danish procurement impact.
options: Prioritize EU standards and interoperability bodies as the primary leverage point, positioning Danish concerns as part of a collective European resilience and portability agenda, with the expectation that EU requirements will then cascade into member-state procurement decisions including Denmark, using European consensus to avoid the appearance of unilateral Danish resistance to platform providers.

Treat EU engagement as supplementary to Danish procurement, timing it to feed demonstrator results and feasibility findings into European working groups during phase three, with the goal of informing future European standards without depending on EU alignment for Danish success.

Launch parallel negotiations with EU bodies and Danish authorities simultaneously in phase two, using EU engagement as a negotiating wedge in Danish procurement discussions (signaling that Denmark is aligned with emerging European requirements) while also using Danish procurement momentum to accelerate EU standards discussion.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between institutional leverage and timeline mismatch. EU standardization can move slowly (2–3 year cycles), and Denmark's AltID decisions may be finalized before European consensus emerges. Conversely, if Denmark unilaterally pushes back against incumbents without EU backing, it risks being characterized as protectionist or anti-competitive. None of the options address the risk that EU standardization bodies will eventually align with platform-vendor preferences due to lobbying volume, in which case Danish reliance on EU standards for legitimacy becomes a liability.
levers.lever_classification: governance — whether to prioritize EU standards alignment as a primary goal or a secondary outcome, and when to engage European bodies

levers.name: Phase-gate continuity and scope-contraction contingency
levers.consequences: Deciding what level of institutional or technical progress is required to unlock phase-two and phase-three funding, and what the contingency plan is if those gates are not met, determines whether the project pivots gracefully or defaults to over-commitment. Stringent gates (e.g., 'formal written AltID governance buy-in required to unlock phase two') may prevent progress if institutional engagement is slow; loose gates may result in poor-quality phase-two work that damages credibility. This lever also determines whether phase three proceeds as originally scoped (full procurement intervention) or de-scopes to publication and EU engagement if Danish procurement traction is weak by month 16.
options: Set phase-two unlock criteria narrowly: phase one must deliver a published feasibility report and at least one formal institutional response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operator, with phase-three scope remaining at full procurement scale but de-scoping automatically to standards and coalition work if AltID governance has not engaged by month 16.

Establish moderate phase-gate criteria requiring phase-one publication plus demonstrated coalition strength (10+ credible institutional stakeholders signed on to the platform-neutrality rationale), with phase-three funding contingent on either securing formal AltID engagement by month 15 or assembling a credible EU standards pathway by month 14.

Front-load phase-one deliverables conservatively, unlocking phase two unconditionally once the feasibility report is published and peer-reviewed by external security experts, allowing phase-two work to proceed in parallel with ongoing institutional engagement, and reserving phase-three scope decisions (procurement versus standards-only) to month 14 based on real-time institutional traction.
levers.review_lever: The tension is between financial discipline and risk-of-abandonment. Stringent gates reduce wasted spending but may cause false-negative exits if institutional engagement is inherently slow (government processes can take 4–6 months for formal responses). Loose gates or unconditional phase-two unlocking preserve momentum but can result in sunk costs on demonstrators if institutional traction fails. None of the options explicitly budget for or plan the team restructuring, time reallocation, or publication strategy required if phase three must de-scope from procurement intervention to standards and coalition work.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how tightly to gate funding releases to institutional progress, and what contingency plan to execute if procurement engagement stalls

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a structural digital-sovereignty crisis: MitID's authentication model depends on Apple and Google platform integrity signals, leaving critical public services vulnerable to supplier concentration, geopolitical disruption, and policy changes beyond Danish control. The project's core tension is between the entrenched convenience and security assurance of the iOS/Android duopoly and the strategic necessity of a certified fallback path and platform-neutral procurement requirements. The chosen levers target this tension across three dimensions: (1) how to frame the problem to unlock political will (resilience and continuity framing, rather than ideology); (2) how to sequence technical evidence and standards pressure to create procurement leverage before AltID hardens; and (3) how to manage institutional resistance and incumbent operator responses by establishing credible alternative architectures without frontal attacks on existing systems. These levers navigate the fundamental conflict between short-term operational convenience and long-term strategic autonomy.

levers.name: Fallback authentication architecture selection and fallback-first messaging
levers.consequences: Choosing whether the fallback path leads with browser-based authentication, hardware tokens, or a hybrid approach shapes both technical credibility and political messaging. A fallback-first framing (citizens should expect platform-neutral access as default, not emergency backup) reverses the psychological anchor from 'what if Apple/Google disappear?' to 'why are we comfortable with a single-vendor authentication path for essential public services?' This reframing may accelerate policy traction but risks alienating MitID operators who see fallback as implicit criticism of current design.
options: Develop a hardware-token-based fallback path that requires no dependency on any mobile operating system, presenting it as the 'sovereignty-grade' option that aligns with Denmark's critical-infrastructure resilience standards and appeals to continuity-planning stakeholders and elderly or non-smartphone users.

Design a browser-based fallback using standard web authentication (FIDO2/WebAuthn) that functions identically across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge, framing it as the minimum sufficient alternative that leverages existing browser standards and sidesteps OS-level vendor lock-in while remaining accessible to most citizens.

Engineer a dual-stack fallback where GrapheneOS or a comparable privacy-respecting Android variant serves as the primary demonstrator platform, but the fallback anchor is explicitly web-first and device-agnostic, signaling that the security model does not depend on Google's integrity APIs even when the primary interface is Android-based.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is whether fallback design should optimize for maximum independence (hardware tokens) or maximum adoption (browser-based), but none of the options address the regulatory complexity of requiring citizens or public-sector operators to maintain two separate authentication paths in production. A fallback that demands training, device acquisition, or workflow change for everyday use will remain unused until crisis forces adoption — at which point familiarity and muscle memory are absent.
levers.lever_classification: product — what authentication architecture anchors the platform-neutral access path

levers.name: Demonstrator environment fidelity and production-simulation scope
levers.consequences: The demonstrator must be credible enough to convince procurement stakeholders and EU technical working groups that platform-neutral authentication is feasible, but scope-creep toward production-like complexity (full MitID integration, real banking workflows, live public-sector systems) would exhaust budget and blur the project's advocacy mission. Higher fidelity attracts technical credibility but invites integration delays and incumbent operator objections; lower fidelity risks dismissal as 'not representative of real constraints.' The decision also shapes whether external security auditors will certify the demonstrator as meaningful evidence.
options: Build a closed-loop demonstrator that simulates a complete Danish public-sector identity-provider environment (certificate issuance, authentication events, revocation checks) using SAML2 and OpenID Connect against a synthetic citizen database, ensuring all trust signals (timestamps, key rotation, audit logs) are authentic and auditable without requiring integration with live MitID or banking infrastructure.

Construct a lightweight proof-of-concept that authenticates against a minimal OIDC provider, deliberately avoiding simulation of real-world edge cases (revocation latency, key compromise, federation complexity) in order to keep scope bounded and demonstrate the core principle that web standards can replace proprietary platform integrity checks, then explicitly document which real-world scenarios the PoC does not cover.

Negotiate limited read-only access to a MitID test environment or request a formal technical collaboration with the MitID operator to build a demonstrator that replicates genuine MitID workflows and trust assumptions under controlled conditions, positioning this as a mutual-interest validation exercise rather than a competing product.
levers.review_lever: Demonstrator fidelity and scope interact with financing constraints and institutional access constraints in ways the options do not surface. A fully realistic simulation may require cryptographic material, test-identity databases, or operational knowledge that the MitID operator will not share with a project framed as an alternative. Conversely, a simplified PoC will be dismissed as 'doesn't reflect production complexity' — the objection will shift, not disappear.
levers.lever_classification: product — how faithfully the demonstrator replicates production authentication constraints

levers.name: Regulatory certification pathway and third-party assurance model
levers.consequences: The demonstrators must be technically sound but do not require production certification under eIDAS or NSIS. However, external validation — whether from a recognized security lab, academic institution, or independent standards body — dramatically increases perceived credibility with procurement officers and EU technical committees. Investing in third-party review diverts budget from additional technical development but creates a credible independence barrier that incumbent operators cannot easily dismiss. Skipping external review keeps budget flexible but leaves the project vulnerable to claims of 'self-interested technical claims.'
options: Commission a formal security review and interoperability assessment from a Danish or Nordic third-party auditor with eIDAS expertise, explicitly scoping it to validate that the demonstrator implements FIDO2 and OpenID Connect correctly and that the fallback architecture does not introduce new attack vectors, then publish the audit report as a standalone document that procurement teams can reference independently.

Engage academic researchers from a recognized Danish or European university with a digital-identity research group to co-author a peer-reviewed technical publication that analyzes the demonstrator's architecture against formal threat models and procurement requirements, positioning academic peer review as the assurance model rather than commercial certification.

Seek informal endorsement and technical feedback from individual experts on the relevant EU and ETSI technical working groups (such as EIOPA or the eIDAS2 working group) rather than commissioning a formal review, using their names and feedback as social proof while avoiding the cost and timeline of a formal audit, accepting that this approach carries reputational risk if the experts later distance themselves or are perceived as biased.
levers.review_lever: The choice between formal third-party certification, academic co-authorship, and informal expert endorsement shapes both credibility and procurement adoption, but none of the options account for the fact that certification or endorsement from any body will be contested by incumbent operators as either insufficient rigor or inherent bias. An external review does not inoculate the project against claims of being 'ideologically motivated' — it may instead invite scrutiny of the reviewer's prior positions.
levers.lever_classification: governance — what external validation model provides demonstrator credibility

levers.name: AltID engagement model and procurement-influence timing
levers.consequences: AltID is Denmark's EU-mandated eIDAS2 wallet rollout, with procurement and design decisions likely to harden by mid-2027. Early and direct engagement with the AltID program (months 9–12) creates opportunities to influence requirements language but risks being perceived as competing or undermining the program; late engagement (after month 18) allows the project to present finished demonstrators and evidence but may arrive after critical procurement decisions are locked. The timing also interacts with whether the project frames itself as 'improving AltID' versus 'creating a parallel resilience pathway,' a distinction that shapes how AltID stakeholders receive engagement offers.
options: Initiate formal dialogue with the AltID program office by month 10, positioning the project as a technical risk-assessment partner that is validating platform-neutrality and fallback-access feasibility on behalf of the program rather than as a critic, and offering to share demonstrator code and findings under NDA if doing so accelerates AltID's internal evaluation of non-Apple/Google dependencies.

Present the project's findings and demonstrators as external evidence for procurement language at the formal AltID design-review gate (anticipated around month 15–16), using published reports and working demonstrators to advocate for inclusion of platform-neutrality or fallback requirements without seeking early operational collaboration with the AltID team, accepting that late entry reduces influence but maintains independence.

Establish a standing technical working group (invited by the project, co-chaired with AltID and relevant public-sector stakeholders) that meets quarterly from month 8 onward to discuss platform-neutrality, resilience, and fallback access in the context of AltID requirements, explicitly positioning this as peer-to-peer technical governance rather than advocacy, and using the group to build consensus on shared definitions of what 'platform-neutral' means in procurement terms.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between early engagement (which influences design but risks co-option or politicization) and independent delivery (which preserves autonomy but may miss procurement windows). None of the options acknowledge that AltID has strong incumbent-operator stakeholders (MitID, commercial integrators) who may actively resist platform-neutrality language regardless of technical evidence, and that the project's engagement model does not control how AltID program leadership chooses to respond to such resistance.
levers.lever_classification: execution — when and how the project engages with AltID procurement decision-making

levers.name: Coalition narrative alignment and privacy-versus-resilience framing priority
levers.consequences: The project's coalition spans privacy advocates (who oppose Apple/Google as surveillance vectors), security researchers (who prioritize fallback robustness and supply-chain risk), contingency planners (who focus on continuity-of-service and single-point-of-failure elimination), and procurement reformers (who emphasize competitive neutrality and supplier concentration risk). These constituencies have different primary motivations and different language for public communication. Emphasizing privacy risks may alienate public-sector operators who view current MitID security as adequate; emphasizing resilience and continuity appeals to procurement and institutional-risk stakeholders. The choice shapes which political and institutional doors open first.
options: Lead all external messaging with digital-sovereignty, resilience, and continuity-of-service framing, positioning the project as a response to procurement and institutional risk management rather than ideology or privacy critique, explicitly deprioritizing privacy concerns in public statements while privately ensuring the coalition includes privacy advocates whose input shapes technical design.

Integrate privacy, resilience, and competitive-neutrality arguments in parallel throughout all public communications, presenting them as three independent and complementary reasons for platform-neutral access, accepting that this creates a more complex message but may appeal to a broader range of institutional stakeholders and avoid the appearance of a narrowly ideological campaign.

Target messaging separately by audience: resilience and continuity framing for government procurement and contingency-planning channels, privacy framing for civil-society and parliamentary committees, and competitive-neutrality framing for EU-level technical working groups and standards bodies, with the coalition agreeing on a unified private position that acknowledges all three motivations while communicating selectively based on institutional context.
levers.review_lever: The choice among narrative frames controls what institutional stakeholders pay attention, but it does not address the fundamental opposition from Apple and Google themselves, who may respond to any framing (resilience, privacy, competition, or procurement) with arguments about security, user choice, and market leadership. Coalition message discipline also assumes internal agreement on what framing is most effective — in reality, privacy advocates and procurement officers may resist alignment, and the project's governance of coalition messaging is opaque to external observers.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — what framing priority shapes coalition messaging and institutional outreach

levers.name: EU standards leverage and European Digital Identity Wallet alignment strategy
levers.consequences: Denmark cannot unilaterally require European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW) implementations to abandon iOS/Google dependencies; EDIW standards are set by EU bodies with broader political and commercial interests. However, the project can propose that EDIW technical specifications include optional provisions for platform-neutral access and certified fallback authentication, or can advocate for these provisions through relevant EU working groups (EIOPA, eIDAS2 coordinating group). Success here amplifies pressure on Danish procurement (because AltID must be EDIW-compliant) and creates political cover for advocating platform-neutrality domestically. But EU-level traction requires the project to navigate diplomatic and technical consensus processes that operate on timelines longer than 24 months and are subject to intense commercial and political lobbying.
options: Conduct a formal analysis of EDIW technical specification gaps and interoperability requirements related to platform-neutrality and fallback access, then submit a detailed technical position paper to the ETSI eIDAS2 expert group and the EU Digital Identity coordinating bodies, positioning the project as contributing external research to EU standards development rather than as a Danish pressure campaign, and request explicit feedback on whether EDIW v1 or v2 standards can accommodate platform-neutral wallet implementations.

Negotiate direct engagement with one or more European Digital Identity Wallet reference-implementation projects (such as those funded under Horizon Europe or related EU digital-identity initiatives) to include the Danish project's demonstrator code or technical findings as a contribution to European wallet architecture research, leveraging EU research collaboration channels rather than formal standards processes to influence implementation practice.

Propose a dedicated EU research or coordination workstream focused on 'resilience and platform-neutrality in digital identity' under existing EU digital-governance forums, with the aim of establishing a shared baseline definition of what platform-neutral access means across member states and of creating a formal consensus that EDIW standards should support (but not mandate) fallback authentication mechanisms compatible with non-Apple/Google platforms.
levers.review_lever: EU standards and EDIW alignment are necessary for Denmark to claim a broader European context for platform-neutrality requirements, but all three options assume that EU technical bodies and commercial stakeholders (including major app-store operators and device manufacturers with significant EU market share) will engage constructively on this issue. In reality, EU standards processes face intense lobbying and move slowly; the project's 24-month timeline does not align with EU consensus-building rhythms.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how the project leverages EU standards bodies and EDIW development to amplify platform-neutrality advocacy

levers.name: Phase-gating contingency and de-scoping protocol if institutional traction stalls
levers.consequences: The project's phase-gate structure (funding released conditionally based on phase-one deliverables) creates discipline but also creates a decision point in month 8 or 9 where the project team and funders must assess whether phase two and three funding is justified by institutional receptiveness and demonstrable demand. If AltID remains uninterested, if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen does not formally engage, or if the coalition remains small and politically marginal by month 8, continuing to full project scope (three phases over 24 months, 10.5 million DKK) may represent poor capital allocation. The protocol for deprioritizing or de-scoping phase three (procurement intervention) versus maintaining phase two (demonstrator development) and phase three (publication and EU engagement) shapes how the project manages the reality of institutional inertia.
options: Establish a formal gate-review at month 8 where the project's advisory board (including representatives from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, academic digital-identity experts, and coalition leads) jointly assesses institutional traction and provides a binding recommendation on whether to proceed with phases two and three, with explicit thresholds for what counts as 'sufficient engagement' (e.g., a formal written response from one government entity, or a coalition of at least three independent organizations publicly supporting platform-neutrality language).

Define phase two and phase three as independent, with phase two funding guaranteed (demonstrators will be built regardless of institutional response) but phase three funding contingent on demonstrating concrete interest from AltID, EU working groups, or other procurement-decision bodies by month 14; if no such interest materializes, pivot phase three toward publication, consolidation of research findings, and long-term coalition stewardship rather than active procurement intervention.

Implement a rolling contingency model where the project team conducts informal stakeholder-sentiment checks at months 6, 10, 14, and 18, with the option to shift focus from Danish procurement (AltID) toward EU standards development or academic publication at any of these gates if institutional Danish-government engagement appears unlikely, avoiding a dramatic phase-three rescope but allowing gradual reorientation.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between the discipline of a pre-defined gate (which forces a clear decision) and the flexibility of rolling contingency assessment (which allows course correction but may obscure when the project has truly failed to gain traction). None of the options address the political difficulty of publicly acknowledging that a project funded to influence Danish procurement has failed to do so, which may create pressure to claim success even if institutional adoption is minimal or if the project's influence over AltID design is actually invisible.
levers.lever_classification: governance — what contingency protocol governs funding release and project pivots if institutional receptiveness is weak

levers.name: Reframing vector: resilience and contingency versus ideology
levers.lever_classification: governance — which narrative framing drives political and institutional buy-in
levers.consequences: By leading with supplier-concentration risk, continuity-of-service exposure, and procurementauthority interests rather than privacy or geopolitical narratives, the project gains traction with civil-protection, treasury, and digital-governance stakeholders who control infrastructure decisions. This reframe also deflects incumbent resistance framed as "ideological" and anchors the case in measurable institutional risk. The trade-off is that resilience framing may dilute the privacy and democratic-accountability case, potentially leaving MitID's structural surveillance characteristics unaddressed in final procurement requirements.
options: Lead all phase-one messaging with quantified supplier-concentration risk (e.g., 'two vendors control authentication for 98% of citizen-critical services'), targeting treasury, contingency planning, and digital-governance officials before engaging civil-society advocates.

Develop dual messaging: one for institutional and procurement audiences emphasizing resilience and fallback requirements, and a separate narrative for civil society and media emphasizing privacy, democratic accountability, and reduced lock-in, coordinated through the coalition.

Position the issue primarily through the lens of eIDAS2 compliance and EU-mandated interoperability, framing platform-neutrality as a technical requirement derived from Brussels rather than a Danish political demand.
levers.review: The core tension is whether reframing toward resilience weakens the privacy and sovereignty argument by making it seem merely technical rather than politically significant. The options all assume that institutional and civil-society audiences can be reached simultaneously without contradiction; in practice, emphasizing contingency planning and supplier risk to treasury officials may inadvertently signal to incumbent operators that platform-neutral fallbacks are merely insurance—nice-to-have rather than mandatory—which undercuts procurement pressure.

levers.name: Coalition composition and advocacy sequencing
levers.lever_classification: governance — which stakeholder groups are mobilized and in what sequence
levers.consequences: The project's ability to embed platform-neutrality language into procurement and standards depends entirely on demonstrating that multiple constituencies (civil society, academic security experts, contingency planners, sympathetic legislators, and EU-aligned policy makers) view the issue as serious. Early coalition building (phase one) establishes credibility with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID operators before phase three procurement intervention. The trade-off is that broader coalitions are slower to mobilize and may dilute the project's technical and policy message to accommodate diverse stakeholder interests.
options: Build a narrow coalition of elite security researchers, privacy advocates, and one or two forward-thinking Folketinget members during phase one, then rapidly expand public visibility in phase two using demonstrators and media to create political pressure before phase three procurement engagement.

Establish a formal, multi-stakeholder governance board (including civil-society organizations, academic institutions, industry associations, and government observers) from month one, prioritizing consensus-building over rapid action to signal legitimacy and institutional endorsement to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

Sequence advocacy through existing Danish digital-governance committees and EU-level working groups first, allowing institutional channels to build momentum before civil-society mobilization, thereby reducing perceived radicalism and increasing adoption likelihood in procurement.
levers.review: The tension is between insider legitimacy (formal boards, institutional channels) and disruptive visibility (media, public pressure). None of the options adequately address the risk that a slow, consensus-oriented coalition will find MitID operators and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen unmotivated to change before AltID architecture is finalized; conversely, a fast external campaign may be dismissed as activism rather than credible technical input.

levers.name: Demonstrator scope and proof-of-concept boundaries
levers.lever_classification: execution — how demonstrator scope and validation rigor are balanced
levers.consequences: Non-production demonstrators (phase two) serve as credible evidence that standards-based, platform-neutral authentication is technically feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google. Demonstrators against simulated environments, using open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect, GrapheneOS), provide the technical ammunition for phase three procurement language. The trade-off is that demonstrators remain deliberately non-production; if institutions perceive them as incomplete prototypes rather than feasibility evidence, they may be dismissed as insufficient to guide real procurement decisions.
options: Develop a single, comprehensive mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS that closely mirrors real-world MitID interaction patterns, including encryption, multi-factor support, and mock integration with simulated banking and tax-filing portals, accepting increased scope and timeline risk to maximize credibility.

Produce two lean, modular demonstrators—one mobile-based FIDO2/OpenID stack on GrapheneOS and one browser-based WebAuthn fallback—each deliberately kept minimal to confirm feasibility and reduce complexity, emphasizing generalizability over feature completeness.

Commission an external, independent security audit of the demonstrator architecture and source code to certify that standards-based approaches meet Substantial-level eIDAS requirements, prioritizing third-party validation over in-house implementation.
levers.review: The tension is between demonstrator completeness (which increases institutional credibility but risks scope creep and delays) and demonstrator minimalism (which speeds delivery but may be perceived as toys or proofs-of-concept rather than evidence for procurement decisions). None of the options squarely address whether demonstrators should attempt certification-level assurance or remain deliberately pre-certification, which affects how seriously institutions take the findings.

levers.name: AltID engagement strategy and timing of procurement intervention
levers.lever_classification: governance — timing and leverage model for procurement influence
levers.consequences: Phase three success hinges on embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into AltID procurement, standards, or design documents. Timing is critical: AltID architecture is likely hardening in 2027–2028, creating a narrow window for influence. Early engagement (phase two) establishes relationships and surfaces obstacles; late engagement (phase three) allows demonstrators to inform procurement but risks missing architecture-lock-in. The trade-off is between establishing early trust with AltID stakeholders (which may constrain the project's ability to be critical) and waiting for demonstrator credibility (which may arrive too late to influence design).
options: Engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership as formal project partners from month six onward, involving them in demonstrator design and phase-one findings, positioning the project as supportive infrastructure rather than external pressure.

Maintain deliberate distance during phases one and two, building independent credibility through publications and coalition, then enter AltID procurement discussions in month 18 with demonstrators, feasibility reports, and EU-aligned standards language as external leverage rather than insider collaboration.

Parallel-path engagement: establish informal working relationships with AltID technical teams and EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups during phase two, sharing findings in non-binding forums while preserving the formal independence and public coalition for phase three pressure.
levers.review: The core tension is whether early partnership risks co-optation (AltID leadership absorbs recommendations without binding commitment) or early distance risks irrelevance (decisions are already made by the time external pressure arrives). None of the options adequately account for the likelihood that AltID procurement cycles are already determined and that formal change requests may face automatic rejection unless political pressure from multiple directions (EU, civil society, treasury) converges simultaneously.

levers.name: EU-level standards alignment and European Digital Identity Wallet positioning
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how European standards and supranational regulatory frameworks are leveraged
levers.consequences: Aligning the project's platform-neutrality advocacy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards and eIDAS2 interoperability requirements amplifies Danish influence by embedding the case in supranational regulation rather than relying on Danish institutional change alone. EU-level engagement also creates precedent for other member states and reduces the perception that platform-neutral fallbacks are idiosyncratic Danish demands. The trade-off is that EU-level standards work moves slowly (24–36 month cycles for formal amendments) and may be co-opted by incremental language that does not mandate fallback mechanisms.
options: Secure direct engagement with the European Commission's digital identity working groups and the EIDAS2 standards revision process by month twelve, submitting technical input on platform-neutral access and fallback authentication, treating the EU as the primary intervention point and positioning Denmark as implementing supranational requirements.

Develop a joint position paper with other Nordic or EU member states expressing shared concerns about platform lock-in in digital identity, building multilateral coalition pressure before approaching Brussels, thereby increasing influence on EU standards development.

Produce a standalone technical report on platform-neutral fallback authentication aligned to eIDAS Substantial-level requirements, publish it through academic and EU policy channels, and allow it to circulate as independent evidence without formal project branding, permitting broader adoption and reducing institutional resistance.
levers.review: The tension is between direct supranational influence (which requires sustained EU-level presence and relationships) and ambient diffusion of technical evidence (which spreads ideas but lacks coordination). None of the options adequately address whether EU-level engagement distracts from Danish institutional change or strengthens it; there is also a risk that EU standards language remains purely aspirational and does not translate into binding procurement requirements at member-state level.

levers.name: Financing contingency and phase-gating discipline
levers.lever_classification: operations — how funding releases and phase-gating discipline constrain execution
levers.consequences: The project budget (10.5 million DKK across three phases) is front-loaded risk: phase one funding is unconditional, but phases two and three are conditional on documented progress and institutional engagement. This creates forced decision points and prevents funding drawdown on weak results. Stringent gating discipline prevents scope creep and money burning on low-probability outcomes. The trade-off is that conditional funding may starve phase two and three if phase one produces incomplete coalition buy-in or if AltID engagement remains informal, leaving demonstrators unfinished and standards language unachieved.
options: Establish a formal, externally chaired project review board at month eight (end of phase one) that makes a binding decision whether phases two and three are funded based on documented feasibility evidence, institutional engagement, and coalition strength; if conditions are not met, wind down the project and publish findings.

Secure the full 10.5 million DKK in phase one as a single conditional budget contingent on phase one deliverables, allowing internal project management to reallocate funds across phases two and three based on institutional momentum, reducing external gate reviews but increasing execution risk.

Structure funding as three tranches released against specific phase-exit criteria (phase one: published feasibility report and two formal institutional engagement letters; phase two: two working demonstrators and documented AltID/EU engagement; phase three: procurement or standards language inclusion), with a clear wind-down protocol if gates are not met.
levers.review: The core tension is between adaptive funding flexibility (which allows pivoting toward unexpected opportunities) and disciplined contingency planning (which prevents resource waste on dead-end paths). None of the options directly address what happens if institutional inertia defeats the project's goals by month 20—whether the project pivots toward publication and EU-level influence or admits failure and closes. The current framing assumes conditional gates will transparently reveal dead ends; in practice, institutional stakeholders may provide ambiguous signals that allow the project to continue despite weak progress.

levers.name: Risk escalation and incumbent operator response strategy
levers.lever_classification: governance — how the project anticipates and responds to incumbent operator resistance
levers.consequences: MitID operators and platform vendors (Apple, Google) have strong incentives to resist platform-neutral alternatives: platform-neutral fallbacks imply reduced exclusive dependence on their distribution, trust signals, and telemetry. By month ten or twelve, operators may become actively obstructive—withholding interoperability information, lobbying against procurement changes, or arguing that fallback authentication introduces compliance risk or user-experience degradation. The project must anticipate and prepare for escalation, including media campaigns, technical counter-arguments, and regulatory lobbying from incumbents. Failure to prepare a public-response and escalation strategy leaves the project vulnerable to being out-maneuvered during phase three.
options: Prepare a detailed 'incumbent response playbook' during phase two that documents likely operator objections (cost, compatibility, security assurance), pre-authors rebuttals, and briefs coalition members on counter-messaging, ensuring rapid, coordinated public response if operators launch campaigns against platform-neutrality procurement language.

Engage MitID operators as early as phase one through formal consultation channels, inviting their input on demonstrator design and fallback-access requirements, positioning platform-neutral fallbacks as supplementary rather than threatening, and reducing likelihood of active escalation through perceived co-optation of their concerns.

Assume active incumbent resistance and design phase three engagement to bypass MitID operators entirely, focusing procurement pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership through political and EU channels rather than attempting consensus with existing platform-dependent vendors.
levers.review: The tension is between consensus-building with incumbents (which reduces overt resistance but may be perceived as capitulation) and public escalation (which mobilizes coalition and media support but risks unified operator pushback that derails procurement influence). None of the options adequately account for the possibility that incumbents will pre-empt the project by accelerating AltID integration with their own platforms, making fallback architectures moot by the time phase three procurement pressure begins.

levers.name: Institutional entry vector and stakeholder sequencing
levers.lever_classification: governance — which institutional door to knock on first and in what sequence
levers.consequences: Deciding whether to approach Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, AltID governance, or EU bodies first shapes both the speed of formal engagement and the likelihood of blocking from incumbents. Early contact with MitID operators may expose the project's intent prematurely and trigger defensive positioning; early EU engagement offers diplomatic cover but delays Danish procurement leverage. This lever determines whether the project operates as a peer-to-peer researcher engagement, a formal proposal through official channels, or a standards advocacy campaign from the outside in.
options: Initiate direct conversation with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's digital-identity working group early in phase one, framing the engagement as resilience planning and continuity risk assessment rather than MitID criticism, with the goal of securing an explicit request for the feasibility report.

Approach AltID governance and procurement teams in parallel with phase-two demonstrator work, positioning the fallback authentication concept as a risk-mitigation requirement for AltID's design rather than a separate competing pathway.

Prioritize EU-level engagement through the European Digital Identity Wallet working group and relevant standards bodies (ETSI, CEN) during phase two and three, allowing Danish framing to feed into broader European interoperability and resilience language before Danish procurement finalizes.
levers.review: This lever assumes that institutional receptivity is stable across the 24-month window, but digital-policy cycles are compressed and government priorities shift with electoral cycles. None of the options account for a sudden political reshuffle that could either accelerate or freeze the entire Danish digital-identity agenda, nor do they address the risk that early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen could trigger an 'internal review' that delays any formal response for 12+ months.

levers.name: Platform-neutrality requirement scope and procurement language
levers.lever_classification: governance — how stringently to constrain digital identity procurement to platform-neutral and fallback-capable design
levers.consequences: Deciding whether to push for 'no exclusive dependency on Apple or Google,' 'mandatory platform-agnostic fallback paths,' or a broader 'supplier diversity and continuity' mandate determines how aggressively the project constrains future identity-stack design. Narrow wording may be easier to negotiate but does not prevent AltID from choosing a single certified platform; broad wording generates stronger resistance from incumbents and may fail at procurement gate. This lever directly shapes what 'success' looks like in phase three and whether the project claims a real policy win or a symbolic endorsement.
options: Draft language requiring that Danish digital identity systems must not depend exclusively on any single commercial platform provider or app-store ecosystem for distribution, integrity attestation, or push-notification delivery, with a mandatory platform-neutral fallback for critical public-sector authentication.

Propose a weaker formulation focusing on 'supplier resilience' and 'continuity planning' that does not explicitly name Apple or Google but creates procurement incentives to evaluate alternative distribution and trust models without mandating exclusion.

Advocate for an EU-wide interoperability and portability standard (aligned with European Digital Identity Wallet requirements) that requires all member-state identity wallets to support migration between platforms and certified fallback mechanisms, making Danish requirements part of a collective European standard rather than a unilateral Danish constraint.
levers.review: The tension is between procurement enforceability and political feasibility. Narrow language is easier to pass but creates loopholes (e.g., 'fallback' can be lip-service, or AltID can comply by adding a weak browser option). Broad language is harder to negotiate and may face legal challenge from affected platforms. None of the options address the post-approval implementation gap: even if language is included in procurement, the operator may interpret 'platform-neutral' minimally or design a fallback so cumbersome that take-up is negligible.

levers.name: Demonstrator credibility and certification scope
levers.lever_classification: execution — how robust and independently validated to make the non-production demonstrators
levers.consequences: Choosing whether demonstrators use production-grade cryptographic libraries and certified components, prototype-grade open-source implementations, or hybrid approaches affects both the technical credibility with skeptics and the implementation timeline. Full certification is impractical within budget and timeline, but too-academic demonstrators will be dismissed as disconnected from operational reality. This lever shapes whether phase-two work produces compelling evidence that platform-neutral design is immediately deployable or merely 'theoretically feasible someday.'
options: Build demonstrators using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on production-validated cryptographic libraries and hardware security modules (HSM), with external security review by an accredited firm, stopping short of formal certification but generating independent audit reports that skeptics cannot dismiss as incomplete.

Develop a lean prototype on GrapheneOS and open-source authentication components, explicitly labeling it as a proof-of-concept and leveraging academic partnerships for peer review rather than commercial certification, emphasizing feasibility and design transparency over production-readiness.

Combine a mobile-oriented demonstrator using commercial-grade FIDO2 stacks with a browser-based fallback using a hardware security token (e.g., FIDO2 USB key) managed by an established digital-identity provider, demonstrating that fallback works with existing procurement-approved suppliers and requires no new certification infrastructure.
levers.review: All three options assume that external review (academic, commercial, or token-provider-backed) will convince procurement officers, but procurement decisions are often driven by political comfort and path-dependency, not technical credibility alone. A well-audited demonstrator may still lose to 'incumbent supplier already certified and operationally supported.' The options also do not address the risk that showcasing a working fallback will shift incumbent pressure from 'fallback is impossible' to 'fallback is a security liability and must not be enabled in production.'

levers.name: Coalition visibility and advocacy messaging
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — whether the coalition operates visibly or through institutional channels, and how to frame the advocacy message
levers.consequences: Deciding whether the coalition operates openly and loudly, coordinates through back-channel institutional relationships, or alternates visibility based on phase will affect both media attention and incumbent defensiveness. High visibility early pressures procurement decision-makers but also triggers industry counter-lobbying and may be perceived as anti-business; low visibility preserves relationship capital but risks being out-maneuvered by incumbent lobby efforts during phase three. This lever determines whether the project frames platform-neutrality as a civil-rights and sovereignty issue, a technical resilience question, or a procurement risk management problem.
